title: Do LLVM Optimization Remarks Add Signal Beyond Compilation Statistics for Low-Budget LLVM Micro-Search?
description: This project is a controlled baseline study, not a new general phase-ordering algorithm. It asks whether normalized LLVM optimization remarks provide useful online signal beyond simple probe deltas and beyond compiler-emitted `-stats -stats-json` compilation statistics in a tightly restricted six-pass, length-at-most-four LLVM micro-search space.
motivation: Recent work already exploits online compiler evidence for phase ordering, especially CITROEN (IPDPS 2025), which uses pass-related compilation statistics inside Bayesian optimization, and ECCO, which uses richer evidence with causal reasoning. That makes a broad evidence-aware optimizer claim unconvincing under this budget. The real gap is a small, falsifiable study of whether the more semantic but sparser optimization-remark stream adds anything beyond probe deltas and prior compiler-emitted statistics when the search budget is tiny.
proposed_approach: Restrict the search space to six LLVM passes (`inline`, `loop-rotate`, `licm`, `loop-unroll`, `loop-vectorize`, `slp-vectorizer`) with maximum sequence length four, starting every method from the same canonical IR plus a fixed warm-up prelude. Run a precondition study for remark and statistics coverage, then compare four matched-budget methods under 18 `opt` evaluations per benchmark: random search, a probe-delta heuristic, a CITROEN-core statistics baseline that fits an online Gaussian-process surrogate on `-stats -stats-json` counters, and a Remark-State Search that normalizes `-pass-remarks*` output into blocker/opportunity states and ranks candidates with a fixed lexicographic rule. The contribution is the positive-or-negative answer to whether remark-derived blocker states add signal beyond compilation statistics in this restricted low-budget LLVM subspace.

related_work.title: A Survey on Compiler Autotuning using Machine Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: The survey motivates low-budget compiler autotuning but predates the recent online-evidence methods that make novelty difficult here.

related_work.title: Optimization Space Learning: A Lightweight, Noniterative Technique for Compiler Autotuning
related_work.venue: Proceedings of the 28th ACM International Systems and Software Product Line Conference (SPLC 2024)
related_work.how_it_differs: OSL is lightweight autotuning, but not an online-evidence comparison between remarks and compilation statistics.

related_work.title: Efficient compiler optimization by modeling passes dependence
related_work.venue: CCF Transactions on High Performance Computing
related_work.how_it_differs: DCO reduces search using pass-dependence structure; this project instead compares different online evidence streams within a much smaller LLVM subspace.

related_work.title: Towards Efficient Compiler Auto-tuning: Leveraging Synergistic Search Spaces
related_work.venue: Proceedings of the 23rd ACM/IEEE International Symposium on Code Generation and Optimization (CGO 2025)
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper shows that careful search-space restriction is already powerful; the present study asks whether remarks add value even after that low-budget restriction.

related_work.title: Leveraging Compilation Statistics for Compiler Phase Ordering
related_work.venue: 2025 IEEE International Parallel and Distributed Processing Symposium (IPDPS 2025)
related_work.how_it_differs: CITROEN is the nearest published neighbor. It uses pass-related `-stats -stats-json` counters inside an online Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization loop with dynamic multi-module budget allocation. This project does not claim to beat full CITROEN; it analytically reproduces CITROEN's statistics-based evidence idea in a tiny matched-budget setting and tests whether remark-derived blocker states add anything beyond that evidence stream.

related_work.title: Protean Compiler: An Agile Framework to Drive Fine-grain Phase Ordering
related_work.how_it_differs: Protean is a broad fine-grain LLVM infrastructure contribution, while this project is a narrowly scoped comparative baseline study.

related_work.title: ECCO: Evidence-Driven Causal Reasoning for Compiler Optimization
related_work.how_it_differs: ECCO is a richer evidence-aware system with causal reasoning and LLM guidance; this project tests whether a tiny interpretable evidence representation is useful before invoking such heavy machinery.

hypothesis: In a six-pass, length-at-most-four LLVM micro-search space with an 18-evaluation budget, normalized optimization remarks will only be worth carrying forward as a baseline if they provide more useful online guidance than both simple probe deltas and a matched-budget statistics-based surrogate built from LLVM `-stats -stats-json` counters; otherwise the study should conclude that compilation statistics already capture most usable low-budget signal.
success_criteria: At least 4 of the 6 fixed passes are remark-reliable, with at least 0.75 benchmark coverage and at least 2 median normalized records on emitting benchmarks.
The remark normalization audit reaches at least 0.75 Cohen's kappa on `family` labels and at least 0.80 parser coverage.
Remark-State Search beats the equally budgeted Probe-Delta baseline on mean LLVM IR instruction-count reduction across the 8-benchmark set.
Remark-State Search also beats or statistically ties the matched-budget Stats-GP baseline while using a simpler, pre-registered symbolic ranking rule.
Any instruction-count advantage for Remark-State Search is reflected in at least one real endpoint, mean binary size or mean runtime, on the fixed 4-program subset.

If remark coverage is too sparse or Stats-GP consistently wins, the paper explicitly reports a negative result and restricts its conclusion to low-budget six-pass LLVM micro-search rather than making broader phase-ordering claims.
